Think Act Observe Answer
A Feynman Curriculum

Agentic RAG.

Production-level understanding in one sitting. Through analogy, code, and active recall — no jargon left unexplained, nothing watered down.

Start reading →
Scroll

Why this exists.

Technical concepts lose fidelity when simplified. This file teaches agentic RAG at full depth — every niche term kept intact, defined inline, and explained through analogy before code.

Ten chapters. Each ~15–30 minutes. By the end, you won't just know what agentic RAG is — you'll have the vocabulary, intuition, and architecture to build one yourself.

✦

Sharing with someone non-technical? Flip the toggle in the top-right (or bottom-right on mobile). Plain-English blurbs appear under technical paragraphs — the main text stays untouched, so your vocabulary never gets watered down.

Table of contents.

Ten chapters, structured to build from first principles to production.

Chapter 01

The problem RAG was built to solve.

15 minWhy retrieval exists
◈ Learning Objective

Understand why retrieval exists at all, and why vanilla LLMsLLMLarge Language Model — a neural network trained on vast text to predict the next token, producing human-like writing from a prompt. aren't enough.

The core insight

LLMs have two kinds of knowledge:

  • ParametricParametric KnowledgeWhat's baked into the model's weights during training — frozen at the moment training stopped. Fast, free, but stale and prone to hallucination. — baked into weights during training. Frozen, outdated, can hallucinate.
  • ContextualContextual KnowledgeInformation you hand to the model inside its prompt. Fresh and accurate, but bounded by the context window — the model's working-memory budget. — whatever you put in the prompt. Fresh, accurate, limited by the context windowContext WindowThe maximum number of tokens (words-ish) a model can read at once. Modern models range from ~8K to ~1M tokens..

RAG = a system that dynamically fills the context window with the right parametric-like information at query time.

Key Concept

Two kinds of memory

Think of an LLM like a student who aced an exam last year. Parametric knowledge is what they memorized then — it's in their head, instantly available, but possibly out of date and sometimes slightly wrong. Contextual knowledge is the open-book notes you hand them right now. RAG is the system that decides which notes to slide across the desk before they answer.

✧ Analogy

An LLM is a brilliant doctor with 10-year-old med school knowledge. RAG is handing them today's patient chart before they diagnose.

Plain English

The AI knows a lot but its knowledge is frozen in time. RAG is the process of looking up fresh, specific info for each question and feeding it to the AI right before it answers — so its reply is based on what's true now, not just what it learned before.

⟡ Exercise

Write down three questions your LLM cannot answer without retrieval. For example:

  • "What did our client file yesterday?"
  • "What's the current interest rate on BofA credit cards?"
  • "What's in the dispute letter I wrote last Tuesday?"
› Feynman Check
Why can't you just train the LLM on all your data?
Answer

Cost, latencyLatencyTime delay between asking and answering. Training adds minutes-to-months; inference adds seconds., staleness, access control, and the fact that new data arrives constantly. Training a model takes days-to-months and freezes a snapshot of your data; retrieval takes milliseconds and stays fresh. You also can't easily apply per-user permissions to weights, but you can apply them to a query.

△ Pitfall

Thinking RAG is about "giving the LLM more data." It's about giving it the right data at the right time.

NextChapter 02 — Regular RAG (The Baseline)→
Chapter 02

Regular RAG — the baseline.

20 minClassic pipeline & its limits
◈ Learning Objective

Build a mental model of the classic RAG pipeline and its limits.

The pipeline

Classic RAG: index once, query many. One-shot retrieval per question.

Key concepts

  • ChunkingChunkingSplitting documents into smaller pieces (usually 200–1000 tokens each) so relevant fragments can be retrieved independently.: breaking docs into pieces (usually 200–1000 tokens).
  • EmbeddingVector EmbeddingA numeric vector (often 384–3072 dimensions) representing text's meaning. Semantically similar text has similar vectors.: turning text into vectors (1536-dim numbers).
  • Vector similarityCosine SimilarityA measure of how close two vectors point in the same direction, ignoring magnitude. Values run −1 to 1; higher = more similar.: cosine distanceCosine Distance1 − cosine similarity. The standard metric for "how semantically different are these two pieces of text?" between query and chunk vectors.
  • Top-kTop-k RetrievalReturn the k chunks with the highest similarity scores. k is usually 3–10.: how many chunks you retrieve (usually 3–10).
Key Concept

What an embedding actually is

Imagine every sentence getting reduced to a point on a giant invisible map. Sentences about "dogs" cluster in one region; sentences about "tax law" cluster in another. When you ask a question, RAG turns your question into a point on the same map and grabs the sentences physically closest to it. That closeness is semantic similarity — not matching words, matching meaning.

✧ Analogy

You're at a massive library. Every book's been reduced to a "vibe signature" (vector). You describe your question as a vibe. The librarian hands you the books with the most similar vibes. You read them and answer.

⟡ Exercise

Map out the pipeline for this question:

"What's our refund policy for enterprise customers?"

Walk through: chunking → embedding → retrieval → generation. Where could it go wrong?

› Feynman Check
Why does regular RAG fail on "Which clients in Texas owe over $10k?"
Answer

Vector similarity finds semantically similar text, not records matching filters. This is a structured query problem, not a semantic one. You need SQL (or a structured query language) to filter by state and balance. An embedding of the question looks similar to sentences about Texas clients and debt — it doesn't actually find which rows match those criteria.

△ The three fundamental limits of regular RAG
  1. One-shot: no re-querying if results are bad
  2. One source: only searches what you embedded
  3. No reasoning: can't combine sources or do multi-step lookups

This is exactly what agentic RAG fixes.

NextChapter 03 — The Agentic Leap→
Chapter 03

The agentic leap.

20 minRetrieval as tool, not step
◈ Learning Objective

Understand what makes retrieval "agentic" and why it's a paradigm shift.

The shift

Regular RAG: retrieval is a step in a pipeline.
Agentic RAG: retrieval is a tool an agentAgentAn LLM that can choose actions (tool calls) in a loop until it decides it has enough information to answer. chooses to use.

The agent loop (memorize this)

THINK → ACT → OBSERVE → repeat, until the agent decides to ANSWER.
  • THINK: the LLM reasons about what it needs
  • ACT: the LLM calls a tool (retrieval, calculator, API)
  • OBSERVE: the LLM sees the tool's result
  • LOOP: repeat until the LLM has enough to answer
Key Concept

Why "the loop" is the whole idea

Regular RAG answers blind — it looks something up once and hopes it's right. Agentic RAG lets the AI pause, look at what it got, and decide whether to dig more. It might look up three different things in sequence, combine them, and then answer. That ability to keep going until the answer is good is the entire difference.

✧ Analogy

Regular RAG = vending machine (one button, one snack).
Agentic RAG = personal chef (asks what you want, checks the fridge, checks the pantry, maybe runs to the store, cooks).

⟡ Exercise

Take this question: "Is our Q3 revenue higher than Q2, and what drove the change?"

Sketch the agent loop:

  1. Think: "I need Q3 revenue and Q2 revenue"
  2. Act: sql_query("SELECT SUM(amount) FROM sales WHERE quarter='Q3'")
  3. Observe: $2.4M
  4. Act: sql_query("SELECT SUM(amount) FROM sales WHERE quarter='Q2'")
  5. Observe: $2.1M
  6. Think: "Higher by $300k. What drove it?"
  7. Act: sql_query("SELECT product, SUM(amount) FROM sales WHERE quarter='Q3' GROUP BY product")
  8. ... and so on until answering.
› Feynman Check
What's the single structural difference between RAG and Agentic RAG?
Answer

The loop. Regular RAG is a straight line; agentic RAG is a cycle with a decision point at the top. Every other difference (multiple tools, re-querying, grounding checks) follows from the fact that the agent can decide to keep going.

NextChapter 04 — The Five Components→
Chapter 04

The five components.

25 minAnatomy of an agent
◈ Learning Objective

Name and understand every part of an agentic RAG system.

The five

ComponentPurposeExample
BrainMakes decisionsClaude, GPT-4, Llama
ToolsCapabilities the agent can invokeVector search, SQL, APIs
MemoryWhat the agent knows so farMessage history + scratchpad
OrchestratorRuns the loopYour code or a framework
EvaluatorJudges if the answer is goodSelf-critique or separate LLM

Deep dive: tools

Tools are described to the agent via schemasTool SchemaThe structured JSON definition of a tool: its name, description, and input parameters. The agent reads schemas to decide which tool fits.. The schema is everything:

JSON
{
  "name": "query_client_records",
  "description": "Query the client database by ID, email, or name. Returns contact info, account status, and dispute history. Use for SPECIFIC client lookups, NOT for aggregate stats.",
  "input_schema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "client_id": {"type": "string"},
      "email": {"type": "string"},
      "name": {"type": "string"}
    }
  }
}

The description is prompt engineering. Bad description = wrong tool picked = wrong answer.

Key Concept

Tools are apps on the agent's phone

The agent has a menu of capabilities — each one described in plain text. "Search the docs." "Query the database." "Send an email." The agent reads those descriptions and picks whichever matches the job, the same way you pick Maps over Uber based on the icon and name. Write fuzzy descriptions and the agent picks wrong. Write sharp descriptions and the agent picks right almost every time.

✧ Analogy

Tools are like apps on your phone. The name + icon + description determines whether you open Maps or Uber when you need to get somewhere. The agent reads the descriptions and picks.

⟡ Exercise

Write tool descriptions for:

  1. A function that searches your company's Slack history
  2. A function that runs Python code
  3. A function that sends an email

Make each description clear about when to use and when NOT to use.

› Feynman Check
Why is the tool description more important than the system prompt?
Answer

The system prompt shapes personality and approach. Tool descriptions shape capability and choice. A well-described tool in a poorly-prompted agent still gets used correctly. A badly-described tool gets misused no matter how good the system prompt. The description is how the agent knows what the tool is for.

NextChapter 05 — Retrieval Patterns→
Chapter 05

Retrieval patterns — the three shapes.

25 minRouter, Sequential, Parallel
◈ Learning Objective

Know the three fundamental patterns and when to use each.

Three patterns for shaping retrieval — pick based on whether sub-queries depend on each other.

Pattern A: Router (single-shot with intelligence)

Agent picks ONE tool per query. No loop.

Use when: questions cleanly belong to one data source.
Example: "What's our refund policy?" → vector search. "How many clients signed up yesterday?" → SQL.

Pattern B: Sequential (the classic loop)

Agent uses tools one at a time, chaining results.

Use when: later queries depend on earlier results.
Example: "Look up client Mo's disputes, then check if any are past the 30-day FCRA window."

Pattern C: Parallel (fan-out)

Agent fires multiple tools at once, merges results.

Use when: queries are independent and you want speed.
Example: "Get our Q3 financials, recent customer reviews, and competitor pricing."

Key Concept

Why there are three, not one

Simple questions need one lookup — you pick the right tool and answer. Complex questions need multiple lookups, where the second one depends on what the first found — you have to wait and chain. Other questions need multiple lookups that don't depend on each other — you fire them all at once to save time. Three shapes, because there are three kinds of dependency between the steps.

✧ Analogy

Router = a receptionist directing you to the right office.
Sequential = a detective following leads one by one.
Parallel = a research team splitting up to cover ground faster.

⟡ Exercise

For each question, pick the pattern:

  1. "What's the weather in Tokyo?" → Router
  2. "Find similar clients to Sarah, then check their dispute success rates." → Sequential
  3. "Summarize today's news, stock prices, and my calendar." → Parallel
› Feynman Check
When would parallel fail where sequential succeeds?
Answer

When Query B depends on the output of Query A. Parallel can't know Query A's result at dispatch time. Sequential waits. Example: "find Sarah's account id, then look up her disputes" — you can't look up disputes without knowing the id first, so parallel fails.

NextChapter 06 — Building One From Scratch→
Chapter 06

Building one from scratch.

45 minCode with Claude's tool-use API
◈ Learning Objective

Write a working agentic RAG system using Claude's tool-use API.

The minimum viable agent

Python
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# 1. Define tools
tools = [
    {
        "name": "search_docs",
        "description": "Semantic search over company documentation. Use for policy, how-to, and conceptual questions.",
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "query": {"type": "string", "description": "Natural language search query"}
            },
            "required": ["query"]
        }
    },
    {
        "name": "query_database",
        "description": "Run SQL against the customer database. Use for specific records, filters, and aggregations. Tables: customers, orders, tickets.",
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "sql": {"type": "string"}
            },
            "required": ["sql"]
        }
    }
]

# 2. Implement tools
def execute_tool(name, inputs):
    if name == "search_docs":
        return vector_db.search(inputs["query"], top_k=5)
    elif name == "query_database":
        return db.execute(inputs["sql"])

# 3. The agent loop
def agentic_rag(user_question, max_iterations=10):
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_question}]

    for i in range(max_iterations):
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=4096,
            system="You are a research assistant. Use tools to gather information before answering. Cite sources.",
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages
        )

        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})

        # Agent wants to use tools
        if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
            tool_results = []
            for block in response.content:
                if block.type == "tool_use":
                    result = execute_tool(block.name, block.input)
                    tool_results.append({
                        "type": "tool_result",
                        "tool_use_id": block.id,
                        "content": str(result)
                    })
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})
        else:
            # Agent is done
            return response.content[0].text

    return "Max iterations reached."

That's a production agentic RAG system at its core. Everything else is optimization.

Key Concept

What stop_reason is telling you

Each time the AI finishes a turn, it reports why it stopped. If it says tool_use, it means "I want to use one of the tools you gave me — here's which one and what to pass it." Your code runs the tool, hands the result back, and the loop continues. If it says end_turn, it means "I'm done — here's my final answer." That single flag is the whole control flow of the loop.

⟡ Exercise

Build this for a real use case:

  • Pick a domain you know (credit disputes, payroll, inventory)
  • Define 2 tools (one semantic, one structured)
  • Test with 5 questions and watch the loop
› Feynman Check
What happens on iteration 3 if the agent asks for a tool that doesn't exist?
Answer

The API returns an error (or your execute_tool dispatcher returns a KeyError). Your code should catch it and feed a tool_result back explaining the tool doesn't exist — the agent will read the error, self-correct, and try a different approach on the next iteration. This is why structured error messages from tools matter: the agent treats them as feedback.

NextChapter 07 — Production Hardening→
Chapter 07

Production hardening.

30 minSeven deadly problems
◈ Learning Objective

Know every failure mode and how to prevent it.

The seven deadly problems

△ 1. Infinite loops

Symptom: agent keeps calling tools, never answers.
Fix: max_iterations cap (5–10), force final answer on limit.

△ 2. Context explosion

Symptom: token count grows each iteration, LLM degrades.
Fix: summarize tool results, truncate old observations, use sliding window.

△ 3. Wrong tool selection

Symptom: agent SQL-queries for conceptual questions, vector-searches for filters.
Fix: explicit "use for X, NOT for Y" in tool descriptions.

△ 4. Hallucinated tool arguments

Symptom: agent invents column names, client IDs, or parameters.
Fix: put schema in system prompt; validate inputs; return clear errors.

△ 5. Cost explosion

Symptom: each query costs dollars, not cents.
Fix: cache tool results; use Haiku for routing decisions; cap iterations aggressively.

△ 6. Stale retrievals

Symptom: agent returns outdated info.
Fix: re-embed changed docs; add timestamps to retrieved chunks; force freshness checks.

△ 7. Poor groundingGroundingAnchoring the agent's answer to evidence it retrieved, with citations. The antidote to hallucination.

Symptom: agent makes up facts despite having tools.
Fix: system prompt requires citations; post-check answers against tool outputs.

Key Concept

Why agents hallucinate even with tools

An agent with tools can still make stuff up. The tools are available, but the model has to actually use them and then ground its answer in what they returned. If the system prompt doesn't insist on that, the model's training takes over and it just generates a plausible-sounding answer. Grounding is the discipline of forcing the answer to cite the evidence.

The production checklist

  • Max iterations capped (5–10)
  • All tool calls logged with inputs, outputs, latency, tokens
  • Tool errors return structured messages the agent can parse
  • Cost per query tracked, alerts on anomalies
  • Evaluation suite of 20–50 gold-standard Q&A pairs
  • Fallback behavior when tools fail
  • Rate limiting on expensive tools
  • Schema validation on tool inputs
  • Timeout on each tool call (don't hang forever)
  • Structured output format for the final answer
› Feynman Check
You ship v1. Users complain answers are "sometimes wrong." What's your debugging order?
Answer
  1. Check logs: did the agent even call tools?
  2. Check tool outputs: did tools return correct data?
  3. Check tool descriptions: is the agent picking the right tool?
  4. Check system prompt: is the agent being told to cite/verify?
  5. Check eval suite: can you reproduce the failure?
NextChapter 08 — Advanced Patterns→
Chapter 08

Advanced patterns.

20 minDemo → product
◈ Learning Objective

Know the patterns that separate hobbyist demos from production systems.

Pattern 1: Query decompositionQuery DecompositionBreaking a complex question into smaller sub-queries that can be answered independently, then synthesizing the results.

Break complex queries into sub-queries before retrieving.

Flow
"Compare CA and TX client dispute success in Q3"
    ↓
Sub-query 1: CA clients, Q3 disputes, success rate
Sub-query 2: TX clients, Q3 disputes, success rate
    ↓
Synthesize comparison

Pattern 2: Multi-agent / specialist agents

An orchestrator delegates to specialized sub-agents.

Topology
Orchestrator
    ├── Legal Agent (knows FCRA, FDCPA)
    ├── Data Agent (SQL, analytics)
    └── Writing Agent (formats responses)

Why: each sub-agent has focused tools, tighter prompts, and its own context.

Pattern 3: Self-correction / reflection

After generating an answer, the agent critiques itself (a reflection passReflection PassA second pass where the agent evaluates its own draft answer against the retrieved evidence and revises if needed.).

Flow
Draft answer → "Does this answer cite sources?" → If no, retrieve more → Revise

Pattern 4: HyDEHyDEHypothetical Document Embeddings — the agent writes a fake answer first, then searches for docs similar to that fake answer. Often more accurate than embedding the question directly. (Hypothetical Document Embeddings)

Before retrieving, the agent writes a hypothetical answer, then searches for documents similar to that hypothetical.

Why it works: answers are semantically closer to docs than questions are.

Key Concept

Why HyDE feels like cheating

When you search the web, you instinctively rephrase your question so it looks more like what the answer would say. ("How do I fix X" instead of "Why is X broken.") HyDE does this automatically — the AI writes a fake version of the answer, then searches for real documents that look like that fake answer. It's smarter than searching with the raw question.

Pattern 5: Adaptive retrievalAdaptive RetrievalThe agent decides per-query whether to retrieve at all — skipping retrieval when it already knows, retrieving when it doesn't. Cuts cost and latency.

The agent learns when to retrieve vs answer from memory.

Decision
"What is 2+2?" → answer directly (no retrieval)
"Who won the 2025 World Series?" → retrieve
"What did I ask you yesterday?" → check memory, not retrieval
✧ Analogy

Demo RAG is a karaoke singer with one mic. Production RAG is a live band — decomposition splits the song into parts, specialist agents are each instrument, reflection is the recording engineer listening back, HyDE is the lead singer rehearsing the melody before the take, adaptive retrieval is knowing when to let silence speak.

› Feynman Check
When would you use multi-agent over a single agent with many tools?
Answer

When (a) you need isolated context windows per domain, (b) different domains need different system prompts, or (c) sub-tasks can run in parallel. Single agent is simpler; multi-agent scales better and handles cross-domain work where one system prompt can't optimize for everything.

NextChapter 09 — Evaluation & Iteration→
Chapter 09

Evaluation & iteration.

15 minMeasuring "good"
◈ Learning Objective

Know how to measure if your agentic RAG is actually good.

Metrics that matter

MetricWhat it measuresHow
Retrieval precisionAre retrieved chunks relevant?Human review or LLM-as-judgeLLM-as-JudgeUsing a separate LLM to score another LLM's outputs. Scalable evaluation when human review is too slow.
Answer accuracyIs the final answer correct?Gold-standard eval set
GroundednessIs every claim backed by retrieved content?Check citations
Tool selection accuracyDid the agent pick the right tool?Log analysis
Latency (p50, p95)p95 LatencyThe 95th percentile response time — 95% of requests finish at or below this value. Key production SLA metric.How fast?Time per query
Cost per queryHow much does each answer cost?Sum token costs
Iteration countHow many loops to answer?Log the loop

The evaluation loop

  1. Build a dataset of 20–50 questions with known-good answers
  2. Run your system on all of them
  3. LLM-as-judge scores each answer (accuracy, groundedness)
  4. Human spot-checks 10% of the LLM judgments
  5. Fix the worst failures → re-run → compare metrics
  6. Never ship without metrics trending up
Key Concept

Why "it feels better" isn't enough

When you change how the AI works, your intuition can't tell you if it's actually better — you need to run the same test questions before and after and compare. Teams that skip this step ship changes that feel good but quietly break things. Always measure; always compare.

› Feynman Check
Your retrieval precision is 90% but answer accuracy is 60%. What's wrong?
Answer

You're retrieving good stuff but the LLM isn't using it properly. Look at: (1) system prompt — are you telling it to ground in retrievals? (2) answer format — is it citing? (3) whether retrieved chunks actually contain the answer or just look similar to the question. Relevant chunks don't always carry the answer — sometimes they carry related but different content.

NextChapter 10 — Shipping to Production→
Chapter 10

Shipping to production.

15 minDemo → deployed
◈ Learning Objective

Know what it takes to go from demo to production.

Pre-ship checklist

  • Eval suite passing at >85% accuracy
  • p95 latency under your SLA
  • Cost per query under budget
  • Observability: every tool call logged
  • Error handling: tools fail gracefully, agent self-corrects
  • Security: tool inputs sanitized, no SQL injection, no prompt injectionPrompt InjectionA security attack where malicious text inside retrieved content manipulates the agent's behavior. Sanitize tool inputs and retrieved text.
  • Rate limits: per-user, per-tool
  • Caching: repeated queries hit cache
  • Fallback: what happens when the LLM API is down?
  • Monitoring: alerts on latency, cost, error rate spikes
Production-grade agentic RAG: orchestrator is the loop; everything else is scaffolding around it.
Key Concept

The orchestrator is the whole product

The fancy 3D world of vector databases and LLM APIs feels like the interesting part. In practice, the piece that makes your system good is the plain orchestration code — the loop, the retries, the timeouts, the logging, the rate limits. That's what separates a weekend demo from something customers rely on.

The final Feynman test

Explain to someone at dinner:

  1. What is agentic RAG in 30 seconds?
  2. Why is it better than regular RAG?
  3. How would you build one this weekend?

If you can do all three without jargon, you're production-ready.

NextFinal Feynman Test — Prove it→

Final Feynman test.

Three questions. Pick the best answer. Wrong answers teach you something too.

An engineer tells you her company has 10 million product descriptions in a vector DB, and her team needs to answer: "Which SKUs from the Q3 launch are outperforming their initial forecast by more than 20%?" What's the first thing you tell her?

The question is a filter and aggregation problem ("outperforming by more than 20%"), not a semantic similarity problem. Vector search returns semantically similar chunks — it doesn't evaluate numeric thresholds. The right architecture is an agent with a SQL tool (for the numeric filter) that can optionally chain to vector search (for descriptive context). Chapter 2's core lesson: structured filters need structured queries. Chapter 5: this is a sequential pattern — SQL first, then optionally enrich with semantic retrieval.

Your agent keeps looping — it calls search_docs, reads the result, then calls search_docs again with a slightly reworded query, and again, burning iterations without answering. What's the most likely root cause?

This is a wrong tool selection problem (Chapter 7, deadly problem #3) manifesting as infinite loop symptoms (#1). Raising max_iterations is a band-aid. The real fix is tool descriptions that tell the agent what each tool is for and when to stop using it — often including an explicit "if search_docs doesn't find the answer in 2 tries, try [other tool] or return that you don't know." Tool descriptions aren't documentation — they're prompt engineering for the agent's decision-making.

In one sentence: what does the stop_reason field in Claude's response control in an agentic RAG loop?

stop_reason is the control flag of the agent loop. After every model response, your orchestrator checks it: "tool_use" means "I want to call a tool — here's which one" so you execute the tool and feed the result back via another turn. "end_turn" means "I'm done — this is my final answer." Without reading this field, you can't run an agentic loop; with it, the whole pattern from Chapter 6 works. It's not a token limit, not a safety filter — it's a state machine signal.

Glossary.

Every niche term from the curriculum. Use this as a reference when friends ask "what's X?"

Adaptive retrieval

The agent decides per-query whether to retrieve at all — skipping retrieval when it already knows the answer, retrieving when it doesn't.

Agent

An LLM that can choose actions (tool calls) in a loop until it decides it has enough information to answer.

Agent loop

The repeating cycle of THINK → ACT → OBSERVE that an agent runs until it decides to produce the final ANSWER.

Chunking

Splitting documents into smaller pieces (usually 200–1000 tokens) so relevant fragments can be retrieved independently.

Context window

The maximum number of tokens a model can read at once — its working-memory budget. Modern models range 8K–1M tokens.

Contextual knowledge

Information you hand the model inside its prompt. Fresh and accurate, but bounded by the context window.

Cosine similarity

A measure of how close two vectors point in the same direction, ignoring magnitude. Range −1 to 1; higher = more similar.

Cosine distance

1 − cosine similarity. The standard metric for "how semantically different are these two pieces of text?"

Embedding (vector)

A numeric vector (often 384–3072 dimensions) representing text's meaning. Semantically similar text has similar vectors.

Grounding

Anchoring the agent's answer to evidence it retrieved, with citations. The antidote to hallucination.

Hallucination

When an LLM generates plausible-sounding but false information — making up facts, citations, or tool outputs.

HyDE

Hypothetical Document Embeddings — agent writes a fake answer first, then searches for real docs similar to that fake answer.

Latency

Time delay between asking and answering. Typically measured at p50 (median) and p95 (95th percentile).

LLM

Large Language Model — a neural network trained on vast text to predict the next token.

LLM-as-judge

Using a separate LLM to score another LLM's outputs. Scalable evaluation when human review is too slow.

Orchestrator

The code that runs the agent loop — managing iterations, tool dispatch, logging, and the max-iteration safeguard.

p95 latency

95th percentile response time — 95% of requests finish at or below this value. Key production SLA metric.

Parametric knowledge

What's baked into the model's weights during training — frozen, fast, free, but stale and prone to hallucination.

Prompt injection

A security attack where malicious text inside retrieved content manipulates the agent's behavior.

Query decomposition

Breaking a complex question into smaller sub-queries answered independently, then synthesizing the results.

Reflection pass

A second pass where the agent critiques its own draft answer against the retrieved evidence and revises if needed.

Reranker

A second-stage model that re-scores the top-k retrieved chunks for relevance, usually more accurate than the initial vector search.

Retrieval router

A pattern where the agent picks one tool per query rather than chaining — fast, no loop, for clearly-typed questions.

Schema (tool)

The structured JSON definition of a tool: its name, description, and input parameters. How the agent knows what the tool does.

stop_reason

Claude API field telling your orchestrator whether the model wants to call a tool (tool_use) or is done (end_turn).

Top-k

Return the k chunks with the highest similarity scores. k is usually 3–10.

Tool

A function the agent can call — vector search, SQL, API, calculator, etc. Described via a schema.

Vector DB

A database optimized for similarity search over high-dimensional embedding vectors. Pinecone, Qdrant, pgvector are common choices.

Ten chapters · Self-contained · Offline-ready

Built as a single-file HTML artifact. Share it. Fork it. Read it twice.

Agentic RAG — A Feynman Curriculum · 2026
